missing_perspectives.what: E-wallets are gateway to credit/insurance/subsidies but also new financial risks
missing_perspectives.why: Analysis focuses on volume and structure, missing how e-wallets shape inclusion/exclusion for bottom 40%

missing_perspectives.what: E-wallets lower formal finance barriers but merchant fees and settlement delays eat thin margins
missing_perspectives.why: Merchant economics invisible in transaction-centric analysis

missing_perspectives.what: Infrastructure gaps and Peninsular-centric e-wallets limit inclusion; state-level Sarawak Pay reflects desire for local accountability
missing_perspectives.why: Geographic dimension mentioned only as adoption gap, not as political/infrastructural exclusion

missing_perspectives.what: E-wallets integrated with social media/BNPL/gig income; fragmentation is feature for platform arbitrage
Lack of formal ID blocks e-wallet access; digital literacy programmes fail without culturally appropriate design
missing_perspectives.why: Foundational exclusion of indigenous peoples not addressed

missing_perspectives.what: More likely to use cash; lower smartphone access and digital literacy

alternative_framings.current: Fragmentation is a problem requiring consolidation
alternative_framings.alternative: Fragmentation may be feature of competitive market benefiting consumers; consolidation reduces innovation
alternative_framings.evidence: Reasoning from competition economics — analysis assumes consolidation desirable without examining welfare trade-offs

alternative_framings.alternative: Gap also reflects rural economy structure — cash dominant for small irregular transactions; e-wallet incentives designed for urban patterns
alternative_framings.evidence: Reasoning — analysis cites 28% but doesn't examine why cash persists

alternative_framings.current: Data sharing is consumer protection issue
alternative_framings.alternative: Data sharing is also competition issue — dominant wallets gain unfair advantage in credit scoring/advertising
alternative_framings.evidence: India UPI debate shows data access can entrench dominant players

alternative_framings.alternative: Losses may reflect strategic investment in user acquisition (Amazon-like)
alternative_framings.evidence: Venture-backed firms often operate at loss to gain market share

international_parallels.lesson: Mandatory national QR standard unified fragmented payments, reduced merchant onboarding costs
Two apps dominate 80% market share — created 'too big to fail' risks; concentration parallels Malaysia's 82% top-3
Real-time transfers between bank accounts and e-wallets reduce need for multiple wallet balances
Success in financial inclusion came from low fees, agent networks, regulatory forbearance — Malaysia's urban focus may miss agent model
UK e-money funds NOT protected by deposit insurance scheme — same gap exists in Malaysia

counter_arguments.counter: Operator losses may be strategic investments by parent companies (TNG backed by CIMB and Ant Group) to capture long-term share
ReDI initiative has registered 22,368 traders nationwide but only 3,229 in East Malaysia as of May 2025 — significant gap not captured by simple urban-rural binary
PDPA 2024 amendments introduced data portability right (effective June 2025) — could allow users to transfer transaction history between providers

missing_facts.fact: E-wallet balances NOT covered by PIDM deposit insurance
BNM imposed RM600,000 fine on TNG Digital in 2023 for failing to screen names against sanctions lists
Government RM100 e-Madani credit (RM1B total) distributed via e-wallets in 2023
PDPA 2024 amendments introduced data portability right effective June 2025
BNM 2025 e-money policy requires issuers to be FOS members for dispute resolution

gender_gaps: No analysis of women's e-wallet adoption rates vs men's or effects on financial autonomy
No mention of how data sharing could affect women in credit scoring (less formal credit history)
economic_gaps: Treats e-wallet market as single entity but Boost has B40 govt subsidy programmes vs TNG urban-centric
No examination of merchant economics — settlement delays, fees, multiple QR codes
No macroeconomic impact of subsidies/cashback on inflation or consumer debt
geographic_gaps: East Malaysia barely mentioned; Sarawak Pay (S Pay Global) operates independently reflecting local control desire
Rural Peninsular vs rural Sabah/Sarawak not disaggregated
No mention of border areas (Kelantan, Sabah) and ASEAN cross-border QR initiatives
overall_assessment: Solid starting point with economic/social/governance lenses but misses class, gender, geography, and consumer protection. Lacks depth on who is excluded (B40, Orang Asli, East Malaysians), how e-wallets affect different actors (merchants, gig workers, women), and consumer protection gaps (no PIDM, fraud comp limits).
